sentences.text: I come from Poland.
My name-- my name was Sol Galek.
Now when I came to America, I changed to Glick.
G-A-L-E-K.
Schlomo.
In Warsaw.
In 1912, August the 8th.
No.
My mother could not take the-- Warsaw, the-- the traffic and everything.
We moved 15 kilometers from Warsaw in a small town.
Blonie, B-L-O-N-I-E.
Blonie.
No, my mother came from Koval, Koval.
K-O-V-A-L.
This was near the German border, Wroclaw, not far from Wroclaw, a big city.
No.
Wroclaw, too.
In Poland, yes.
It was near-- near the-- the German border.
Oh, she met my father through my father's brother.
He know my-- he know-- the family.
He used to travel.
And he introduced my father to my mother.
And they got married after this, a couple months later.
I know my-- well, my brother was born in 1904.
They must have got married about two years before, 1902.
Yes?
No.
He get around-- selling anything.
I don't know-- my father-- he left for America when I was not born yet.
He run away from the Army.
In Poland, you have to go, 21 years old, you have to go to register when you're 18.
You're not allowed to leave.
If-- if my uncle, my grandfather would be alive, he had to pay fine.
Well my father was his brother.
He paid the fine for him, two brothers escaped.
They didn't want to go in the army.
Yes.
Oh--
Oh, yes they-- they come to Chicago.
My own brother-- my own uncle, my father used to tell me, was a professor in college.
Yeah, in Chicago.
Yes, And the other one was a tailor.
Yes.
My uncle who-- the professor was never married.
He got killed by an automobile.
He used to write and send packages to the family.
After he got killed, we don't know what happened.
My father used to wonder what happened to my brother.
And then somebody came to visit in Poland, and told him the story, how your brother got killed.
It was in the papers in Chicago for relatives for them to get the-- the money what he left.
And this brother, got everything, [INAUDIBLE].
And he didn't notify us, nothing.
Oh, yes.
He sent presents for the sisters-- he had three sisters.
My father come 13 brothers-- with 10 brothers and three sisters.
And my-- my grandfather got married twice.
He first wife died.
He had seven children.
And the second one is-- and I don't remember my grandfather.
But I remember my grandmother when I was a baby.
She-- she stayed with my father.
I don't know.
Galek, I don't know what's her name.
I was a child.
I don't know.
Money, money, money he sent.
Money.
Oh, yes.
My father didn't need it.
But his sisters need it.
My father supports the sisters too.
They were still growing.
My father was in the cattle business.
In a-- in a small town.
Yeah, a shtetl.
Oh, he had a lot of cows.
I used to feed them and everything as a child.
He used to have people going to-- every month was in another shtetl a sale.
People come with the cows like a flea market.
And he had people working for him.
He told them what he needs, how many cows.
He got orders from the farmers, and he-- he-- my father dealed with the Prince Radziwill too.
Oh, he has a big farm.
He would not let in nobody to his farm, except, of course, my father.
When my-- when my father's brother got killed by mistake in Europe, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my father was sitting Shiva.
He would not go there, and he sent my mother, with a-- with a salesman.
No, he sold it for the price for the prince ask him.
But the price was very low.
But with this cattles, he sold him pigs.
And my father would not buy pigs.
He was a very religious man.
But he had to buy it.
And my father sold the pigs to the butchers, the Gentile butchers for the same money.
He said, I would not make a dollar on pigs.
He was-- he was a very religious man.
And this I-- I never forget.
No, through the war I hear that the prince-- I don't know what happened.
His wife was alive, I-- when I came back.
And she told the story of what happened.
He was [INAUDIBLE] and everything.
But his wife was still alive.
And-- and his wife, I never forget when we came back through the war, and I-- and when I saw her, she said don't mention I was a prince.
No, no, no--
No, don't mention.
And she worked in the immigration.
But a Communist already.
And she was afraid.
The Communists hated the-- the rich people.
And she said, don't you ever mention you know me.
She was a lovely woman.
But he was-- and she told how he got killed by the Communists and everything.
The first thing the Communists killed all of the rich people.
Oh, he was a--
He sold it to the butchers, and to butchers.
No, no, no.
We went to gentile butchers and everything, yes.
Yeah, he was a--
No, no.
He just--
No.
We have special stables for the cows, for the milk and everything.
And-- he sold which cows he had.
He always had a lot of cows.
Oh, yes.
I used to milk the cows.
My mother never went to the stable.
She never know what a cow means.
Yeah.
I was the only one.
I feed them and everything.
Four brothers.
No.
The other brothers was-- one brother was in-- in business.
My father put him in business.
He bankrupt the business.
And another time--
The bankrupt--
He played-- he was a guy, he don't want to work.
He wants to play pool and this for money.
And later, when he got married, my father put him in a business again, and he bankrupt.
But all the brothers helped him.
He was one-- our brother-- we loved him.
He was-- he was like a father.
When we had trouble, we come to him to tell the trouble.
We didn't tell my father.
He would not care.
No, the younger brothers-- the-- one-- the brother which you see here in the wedding, he was a president in a bank.
He had a bank.
And the other brother was-- open a store.
And the one brother who was got killed-- just died, he had a factory, making slippers and everything.
And I used to deliver them to the stores.
For orders, I used to take orders.
And I used to help him working there.
Oh, yes.
No, my mother's sisters, three sisters lived in-- in the same city where we live.
About 10 minutes-- 15 minutes by bicycle.
I used to-- I used to go by train.
I used to go to school.
I used to took a train.
I have my monthly pass for the train.
I could go all the time.
High school, I went in Blonie, in Blonie, the city.
Elementary school, and later on, I went--
The first five years was a Jewish.
And after five years, you go into a public school, with all mix, all nationalities.
In Blonie, yes.
In Warsaw it was, you know, I tell you in Jewish, a Yiddish Gemeinde, a society for the Jews, they owned the school to teach the Jewish boys engineers, mechanics, tool makers.
And I went to school there.
It was in Warsaw.
I used to go by-- by train.
Oh, yes.
The Jewish organization, like you have a city hall, you know what I mean?
The Jewish, everything the Jewish-- once there was almost a Jewish city.
The religious people, they never spoke Polish.
They speak with the Hasidim, with the payot.
They own all the business.
There was just-- they speak Jewish in the street.
They never-- my-- my father never learned Polish, even his-- his name.
He was a businessman.
And I told him how to sign his name.
He speak to him in Polish.
Sure.
Polish he could speak.
He never went to school, and never wrote Polish.
Even his name, I used to say, Pop, why did you sign his name?
Oh, yes.
He was-- in Hebrew, he was very educated, but not in Polish.
Yeah.
That's right.
It was just like a gymnasium here.
A professional, you learn everything.
You finish there, you were educated.
And you're a mechanic, or an engineer.
Electrical, plumbing.
Oh, yes.
When I was in California, that's-- that's what I-- that's what I took, my-- my profession.
In Philadelphia, I was a photographer.
I took a-- a job as a-- as a janitor.
I didn't speak English.
Yes, but I went to California, in 1976, '75 they convert the apartment to condos, with a rush of people was-- real estate was very high.
And-- and the apartment which I lived, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they sold the apartment to a Canadian company, kind of like Fairview.
They're here in-- in Manhattan, a big real estate business.
And they need a-- a manager.
And I was supposed to move there.
They gave me a relocation and everything.
And I was supposed to move.
And when the guy was looking at my house, making a telephone to the company.
He said the manager, a woman, quitting.
He needs a manager.
And he made me the manager.
He gave me for three months, and I stayed there over 10 years.
What?
No, by myself.
No, it was just for boys.
And Warsaw was just for boys.
Oh, yes.
I had a lot of friends, sure.
No, they lived in Warsaw.
They come from every shtetl there.
And then people from-- who lived in Warsaw.
It was a big school.
No.
No, just me.
Oh, yes.
I have to take a test, sure.
I did it myself.
You know why?
In this small time in shtetl, they used to have stores, pastry stores, and this.
And I used to take orders.
Instead of them going to Warsaw, bring after school I went into the stores, and I bought it.
And they gave me a percentage.
Yes.
And sometimes, I came home from school about 2 o'clock.
And I went back three, four times to Warsaw.
I had a pass.
And I-- instead of them traveling there, they didn't have time, they gave me the orders, and they gave me a percentage.
That's why I made-- that's why I lived and paid everything.
Oh, you have pastry.
You have other kind of stores.
And they need supplies.
Oh yes.
Sure.
Cheesecake, danish, danish or bread.
Yes, they were very delicious, the biggest stores there.
Once it was-- the fifth city in-- in the world.
From Warsaw to the small towns.
And there they gave me an order-- and later on they gave me some percentage.
Yeah, yeah.
That's what I did.
Later on, when I worked with my brother, I helped him.
Oh, it's a-- it's a big story.
When I was a child, I got spinal meningitis, nine years old.
And millions of people-- children died, thousands of children.
And I was crippled.
Do you know what spinal meningitis is?
I was crippled.
I had-- I had like this with a [NON-ENGLISH], and with-- with the-- with the iron on--
On the legs and everything, and I was blind.
And they pronounced me dead.
This is a true story.
I was eight, nine years old.
My younger brother, who you see there in the picture, I didn't walk.
And he didn't walk either.
He followed me.
And my mother's sister lived next to us.
And the doctor went into her, and told her, look, Mrs.
Galek, he's not gonna live.
Why the mother has to see-- he's gonna die.
I want to give him a needle.
That's why I believe in taking life.
I give him a needle.
And my mother find out, and she throw the doctor out.
I don't want to see you.
You want to kill my child.
I do everything to save his life.
Later, when the doctor came in, a couple of weeks later, pronounced me dead.
I was dead.
And you know, in the Jewish faith, the religious people, they put you overnight on the-- on the floor.
And they put candles around.
And the-- and the religious people who take care of the bodies.
In the morning they wash him, and everything, and they take him to the cemetery.
And when they were washing me, he put a mirror in my face, in my mouth.
And he starts to scream, the baby's alive.
They called the doctor.
I just never forget, all my life, and the doctor said, he's alive.
He is gonna go through hell.
And nothing is gonna happen to him.
He's going to be the strongest child in your family.
Yes.
The strongest child in your family.
And he was right.
I went through hell, operations, I heal right away.
And of all my brothers, I'm-- I'm the only survivor.
One died of a heart attack, one die here of cancer.
Oh, it took a long time.
It took a long time.
When I started going to school, I was already 10 years old.
And I was going with the seven years old.
Yeah.
That's right.
But I-- I was so smart in one year, they push me three classes.
That's true.
I was very bright and everything.
But anyway, I survived.
And I always have in my mind what the doctor said.
You can cut me here.
The next day it's healed.
In soccer, after about 11 years, 10 years.
Oh, yes.
I got the bracelets and everything.
And when I was 18 years old, I had to register for the army.
In Europe, you cannot leave.
At 21 years old, like everybody complains, my heart and this-- they didn't want to go in the army.
And I got an A, everything perfect.
I survived.
And I was a soccer player, very famous and everything.
Oh, I didn't graduate.
I went to school there.
After about 14, 15 years old I went to school.
But I didn't graduate.
I quit.
And I help my brother.
I was going at nighttime.
Yes, nighttime, yes.
Not the same school, the same school.
A couple years.
And then I could not afford to go there, I help my brother.
And I went at nighttime to school.
Yeah.
Yeah, yeah.
Not-- not-- not-- not, I didn't have the diploma.
The war came, and I went to the army.
And--
Yeah, 18.
And later when I went to the army, I came out for the army.
I worked with my brother.
And when the war came--
Yeah.
Before the war, yes.
In 1933, yeah.
Oh, in Poland you have to go draft.
18 you have to register.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] 18, you have to register.
And 21 you go for a physical.
And after the physical, then you go in the army.
Yeah.
And I had it good in the army.
I was never religious.
My father was religious.
He wants to make me a rabbi.
And I want to play soccer.
I could not stay.
I was religious.
I went to cheder and everything.
But I was not an orthodox religious.
My-- my mother-- oh, in the middle of the week, it was fine.
But the-- the games was always on a Saturday.
[LAUGHS] And I come back.
My father come back.
And my mother give me the meat, and the lunch.
But he went to shul.
Oh, he slapped me and everything.
But he was a good father.
Oh, about-- before-- before I went to the army.
What?
I played.
Yeah, yeah, yeah.
I played soccer.
In soccer you have to play as a child.
You cannot play when you're older.
After I was recuperating and everything, I start to play.
And when-- when you get to 30 years old, 35, you can't still play.
Here they play longer.
But you have to learn from a child.
Oh, I was interest in playing.
Ha-Po'el-- first we had a working organization, people who work called-- later on, when I went in Warsaw, I joined the Ha-Po'el.
And I played.
I played.
What?
Oh, they-- they were all students, and everything.
I used to go ice skating, and this, and running.
Oh, yes.
We were going together to dancing, and like young people.
Oh, yes.
Oh, yes.
All the Jewish holidays and everything, we used to celebrate very big.
It's not like here.
Our Passover was a week.
Rosh Hashanah was a week, and Yom Kippur.
[NON-ENGLISH] was-- and family was very happy there.
Oh, yes.
I used to sing a lot of songs.
But who remembers?
I almost went to Israel by bicycle before the war.
Yes, my father was-- tried to kill me.
They all-- all my friends went by bicycles.
And they could not go through, you see, Russia would not let him go through Russia.
Russia at the border, you can't go through.
And they-- they took a boat.
And they went-- to-- to-- to the-- later on-- they all went.
I met them after the war.
My friends-- my friends organized Zionists a trip by bicycle to Israel.
To go to there, and they remained there.
They were very-- very for Israel-- they were--
Yeah, sure.
And my father would not let me.
